data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-02T01:33:54.686Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating this .NET Framework code to .NET 8 and need to ensure all Thread.Abort
usage is addressed. Check this code and provide your Thread.Abort migration guidance.

```csharp
public class SimpleWorker
{
    public void RunBatch(List<WorkItem> items)
    {
        var threads = new List<Thread>();

        foreach (var item in items)
        {
            var t = new Thread(() => Process(item));
            t.Start();
            threads.Add(t);
        }

        foreach (var t in threads)
            t.Join();
    }

    private void Process(WorkItem item)
    {
        // simulate work
        Thread.Sleep(100);
        item.Result = ComputeResult(item.Input);
    }
}
```

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about migrating .NET Framework code that uses Thread.Abort to .NET 8. However, looking at the code they provided, it doesn't actually contain any `Thread.Abort`, `ThreadAbortException`, or `Thread.ResetAbort` usage. It just uses `Thread.Start()`, `Thread.Join()`, and `Thread.Sleep()`.

Let me check the available skills - there's a "thread-abort-migration" skill that seems relevant here. The skill description says:

"USE FOR: modernizing code that calls Thread.Abort, catching ThreadAbortException, replacing Thread.ResetAbort..."


"DO NOT USE FOR: code that only uses Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start without any abort, interrupt, or ThreadAbortException usage — these APIs work identically in modern .NET and need no migration."

So this code doesn't actually need the thread-abort-migration skill since it only uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep - which work identically in modern .NET. I should let the user know their code doesn't use Thread.Abort and doesn't require migration for that concern.

Wait, the user asked me to "ensure all Thread.Abort usage is addressed" - they might be asking me to verify if migration is needed. The skill documentation is clear though: don't use it for code that only has Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start, so I can give them a direct answer.
